stem: By Lagrange's theorem the order of a subgroup divides the order of the group. Suppose a finite group \( G \) has order [[g]] and \( H \) is a subgroup of \( G \) of order [[h]]. What is the index \( [G : H] = \dfrac{|G|}{|H|} \)?
distractor_rationales: This subtracts the orders instead of dividing them.
This adds the orders instead of dividing them.
This reports the subgroup order rather than the index.
This reports the group order rather than the index.
This is off by one from the correct index.
This is off by one from the correct index.

stem: A finite group \( G \) has order \( [[nval]] = [[p]]^{[[a]]} \cdot [[q]]^{[[b]]} \). By Lagrange's theorem, the order of every subgroup of \( G \) must be a positive divisor of [[nval]]. How many DIFFERENT subgroup orders are consistent with Lagrange's theorem (i.e., how many positive divisors does [[nval]] have)?
solution: The positive divisors of \( p^{a}q^{b} \) number \( (a+1)(b+1) \), since each divisor picks an exponent from \( 0,\dots,a \) for \( p \) and \( 0,\dots,b \) for \( q \). Here \( (a+1)(b+1) = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the exponents directly instead of adding one to each first.
This adds the exponents instead of using \( (a+1)(b+1) \).
This adds the two factors instead of multiplying them.
This reports \( |G| \) itself, not the number of divisors of \( |G| \).
This is off by one from the correct divisor count.
This is off by one from the correct divisor count.

stem: A finite group \( G \) has order [[nval]], and [[p]] is the SMALLEST prime factor of [[nval]]. By Lagrange's theorem, what is the largest possible order of a PROPER subgroup of \( G \) (that is, a subgroup with \( |H| < |G| \))?
solution: A proper subgroup's order is a divisor of \( |G| = [[nval]] \) that is strictly less than \( [[nval]] \); the LARGEST such divisor is \( |G| \) divided by its smallest prime factor, \( \frac{[[nval]]}{[[p]]} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This is off by one from \( |G| \), not a divisor of \( |G| \) at all in general.
This subtracts the prime instead of dividing by it.
This reports the smallest prime factor itself, not the largest proper divisor.
This reports \( |G| \), which is not a PROPER subgroup's order.
This is off by one from the correct order.
This is off by one from the correct order.

stem: A finite group \( G \) has a subgroup of order [[h]] and a (different) subgroup of order [[k]]. By Lagrange's theorem, both [[h]] and [[k]] must divide \( |G| \). What is the SMALLEST possible value of \( |G| \) consistent with this?
solution: The smallest number divisible by both [[h]] and [[k]] is their least common multiple, \( \operatorname{lcm}([[h]],[[k]]) = \dfrac{[[h]]\cdot[[k]]}{\gcd([[h]],[[k]])} = [[answer]] \).
distractor_rationales: This multiplies the two orders, which is only the lcm when they are coprime.
This adds the two orders instead of taking their lcm.
This gives the gcd, not the lcm, of the two orders.
This uses only the larger of the two subgroup orders, ignoring the other's divisibility requirement.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.
This is off by one from the correct minimum.

stem: A finite group \( G \) of order [[g]] has a subgroup \( H \) of index \( [G:H] = [[k]] \). By Lagrange's theorem \( |G| = [G:H]\,|H| \). What is \( |H| \)?
distractor_rationales: This reports the index itself rather than \( |H| \).
This subtracts the index from \( |G| \) instead of dividing.
This reports \( |G| \) as if \( H \) were the whole group.
This multiplies instead of dividing.
This is off by one from the correct subgroup order.
This is off by one from the correct subgroup order.
